channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Pakistan Wants the World to See It as a Peacemaker. I Want It to Find My Father.
item.description: The world should not forget about Pakistan’s human rights abuses, even as the state tries to overhaul its image.

item.title: U.S. Strikes Iran Over Attack on Hormuz Ship
item.description: Washington said Tehran violated the cease-fire.

item.title: Is Hezbollah Now More an Obligation Than an Asset to Tehran?
item.description: How the war has shifted Iran’s relationship with its proxies.

item.title: The Art That Created Colonialism
item.description: A new exhibition examines how Western art and colonial aggression made each other possible.

item.title: A Fragile Framework Deal for Iran: What’s Next?
item.description: Stylistically, the Islamabad Memorandum fits perfectly into U.S. President Donald Trump’s dealmaking and diplomatic record. It's also extremely fragile.

item.title: Berlin is even worse equipped than Paris for Europe’s heatwave
item.description: Berliners gaze with dread at this weekend’s forecast of 40ºC (104ºF)

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of June 20: Colombia votes, Starmer resigns, and Rutte meets with Trump.

item.title: Why India’s Foreign Minister Went to Mongolia
item.description: Eurasian-Indo Pacific connectivity begins with Mongolia and India.

item.title: Is too much sleep as bad as too little?
item.description: The quest for the optimal amount of shut-eye

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Lessons from the Cuban revolution
item.description: In our bonus edition, Richard Cockett, a senior editor, explains why now is a timely moment to revisit the events that brought Fidel Castro to power

item.title: Has China Really Entered the Three-Carrier Era?
item.description: The Fujian’s Taiwan Strait transit highlights both the progress and the limits of China’s carrier ambitions.

item.title: Drum Tower newsletter: Who is behind China’s latest AI breakthrough?
item.description: Corbin Duncan, our global correspondent, speaks to the Zhipu team on their technical progress, business model and the future of AI

item.title: Tariff-Proof But Not China-Proof: The Geopolitics of India’s Pharma Power
item.description: Washington has spared India’s generics from its new drug tariffs. But the real exposure running through India’s pharmacy lies upstream, in China – and it is the West’s problem too.

item.title: 10 Years After the South China Sea Arbitration: Will ASEAN Remain Silent?
item.description: Despite its vocal support for UNCLOS, ASEAN has not recognized a legal judgment brought under the convention’s framework.

item.title: India’s Drone Production Ecosystem Is Evolving
item.description: New Delhi’s accelerating militarization of drone technologies not only risks deepening existing asymmetries, but will also fuel a regional drone arms race.

item.title: Can China’s Dong Minority Keep Their Unique Cultural Heritage?
item.description: An ambitious university-led research project to document the Dong people’s distinctive architecture is revealing a great deal about this marginalized Indigenous group’s way of life.

item.title: After the Iran War, China’s Middle East Strategy Will Prioritize the Gulf
item.description: China’s top priority is now keeping the Gulf states, and above all Saudi Arabia and the UAE, from drawing closer to Washington.

item.title: Chun Jae-soo’s New Blueprint for Busan
item.description: South Korea’s second largest city will chart the country’s new maritime plan. And its new mayor will lead the way.

item.title: To Not Lose Southeast Asia, the US Must Look to Northeast Asia
item.description: The U.S. may be losing a one-on-one contest with China for influence. But when you add its allies into the mix, the U.S. bloc is still leading the way.

item.title: Taiwan to Build Chip and EV Factories in Poland Replacing China and the US
item.description: In Poland's tech strategy, Taiwan is filling in for projects once envisioned for China and the U.S.

item.title: Is a European Single Market for Energy Such a Good Idea?
item.description: Although textbook microeconomics shows why less energy-market fragmentation would be better for Europe as a whole, that does not mean it would be a good deal for countries producing low-carbon electricity at minimal marginal cost. In fact, if more integration creates free riders, the political backlash could be profound.

item.title: Colombia Swings to the Right
item.description: It’s the latest Latin American country to elect a Trump-backed leader.

item.title: China’s Failed Rebalancing
item.description: When China’s leaders first acknowledged the need to rebalance the economy nearly two decades ago, it seemed like a matter of when, not if. But with the household consumption share of Chinese GDP remaining stubbornly low, officials’ promises to boost domestic demand have lost all credibility.

item.title: The United States Is Now a Guarantor of Last Resort
item.description: The Gulf region has shown it’s ready to carry the diplomatic burden of managing Iran.

item.title: Pakistan Unveils Growth-centric Budget Amid Volatile Geopolitical Landscape
item.description: The government expects the external account to remain stable in FY27, particularly via extended tax incentives for the IT sector, a rise in exports and workers' remittances.

item.title: Crowning a Healthy Populism
item.description: Although recent political mobilizations in Hungary, Albania, and other parts of Central and Eastern Europe could be described as populist and nationalist, they have little in common with the aggressive ethnonationalism that MAGA professes. Could they be a model for a new form of politics?

item.title: Why Rules-Based Orders Fail
item.description: For a brief period after the Cold War, Americans persuaded themselves that the liberal order had become self-sustaining. And yet, any rules-based system elaborate enough to govern and interpret its own operations will eventually confront questions that its rules cannot answer.

item.title: The Facade of the Thai Royalist Intellect
item.description: Far from shoring up support for the Thai monarchy, the deployment of pseudo-academics and influencers is damaging the royalist cause.

item.title: Who Is Winning Africa’s Drone Wars?
item.description: Ukraine isn’t the only battlefield shaping autonomous warfare.

item.title: ASEAN Membership for Timor-Leste
item.description: Doors open, but structural risks remain.

item.title: An Overview of Corruption Investigations in the Philippines
item.description: The current ubiquity of corruption underscores the normalization of impunity in the country's politics, despite recent democratic uprisings.

item.title: US Counterterrorism Aircraft Could Be Surprisingly Useful in a Taiwan War
item.description: Some equipment central for the Global War on Terror could play important roles in a potential conflict against China.

item.title: Analyzing the Human Toll of Myanmar’s Civil War
item.description: Su Mon Thant from ACLED talks about the military's mounting atrocities, and the Arakan Army's recent abuses against the Rohingya.
